entries.intro: A prospector came through {subject_focus_the} last month claiming he found a vein of something he'd never seen before. Blue-green, harder than iron, and warm to the touch.
details: He showed a sliver of it to the blacksmith. She said it scratched her best steel without leaving a mark on itself.
He wouldn't say where the vein was, but he was coming from the northwest and his boots were caked in red clay.
reactions: Three different people have offered to buy the location off him. He turned them all down flat.
He left town the next day heading back the way he came. Haven't seen him since.

statCheck.pass: Red clay like that only sits in one stretch of the northwest hills: the old river gorge near the collapsed bridge. Narrows it down considerably.
statCheck.fail: Half the northwestern hills have clay deposits. Could be anywhere up there.

entries.intro: There's a ruin near {subject_focus_the} that everyone avoids. Last week a piece of the outer wall crumbled and something glinted underneath the stonework.
details: The stones were packed with some kind of metallic thread, woven through the mortar like veins in a leaf.
The thread doesn't tarnish. Whatever alloy it is, it's been in that wall since the ruin was built, and it still shines.
There's more of it deeper in, where the walls are still intact. You can see it peeking through cracks.
reactions: The mason says it would take days to extract without destroying it. And the ruin isn't exactly stable.
I'm no historian, but if they built entire walls with that stuff, they must have had a lot of it.

entries.intro: A fisherman near {subject_focus_the} pulled up a chest in his net. Small, sealed tight with wax, and heavier than it looked.
details: He cracked it open and found a set of coins nobody recognized. Wrong shape, wrong markings, wrong metal.
The coins have a face stamped on one side and a constellation on the other. Not any constellation we know.
reactions: He tried to spend one at the market. The merchant just turned it over in his hands and gave it back.
Personally, I'm more interested in where it came from than what the coins are worth. Someone sealed that chest and put it in the water on purpose.

statCheck.pass: The wax seal had a pattern pressed into it: a wave over a mountain. That's the old mark of the river traders who disappeared two centuries ago.
statCheck.fail: The markings are worn and the metal is unfamiliar. Nothing clicks, no matter how long I look at them.

entries.intro: An old cellar collapsed near {subject_focus_the} after the rains, and it opened up a chamber underneath that nobody knew was there.
details: The chamber has carved walls. Not rough: precise, with repeating geometric patterns that go floor to ceiling.
There's a stone table in the center with slots cut into the surface. Five slots, evenly spaced, each one shaped differently.
The air down there is dry and still. No dust, no cobwebs. Like it's been sealed perfectly for a very long time.
reactions: The owner of the cellar is charging people a silver to look. Smart man.
I went down there myself. It felt like standing in a room that was waiting for something.

entries.intro: Someone found a map scratched into the bark of a dead tree near {subject_focus_the}. Not carved recently: the bark healed over it, then the tree died.
details: The lines show a path from the old well to something marked with three circles. Nobody knows what the circles mean.
There are symbols along the path that match no writing I've seen, but they repeat at regular intervals.
reactions: A few folks followed the path. It leads to a clearing with a flat boulder, and that's where they lost the trail.
Could be nothing. Could be some shepherd's joke from fifty years ago. But those symbols looked deliberate.

statCheck.pass: The three circles match a symbol I've seen in old survey records: it was used to mark underground water sources. Whatever's at the end of that path, it's below ground.
statCheck.fail: The symbols could be anything: trail markers, property boundaries, or just idle carving. Hard to say without more context.
